mission.title: Install and Configure Coredns on Kubernetes
mission.description: CoreDNS is a flexible and fast DNS server that can serve DNS requests over various protocols. Installing CoreDNS allows you to manage DNS data efficiently using plugins, making it a powerful tool for cloud-native environments.

steps.title: Add the CoreDNS Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official CoreDNS Helm chart repository to your Helm setup with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add coredns https://coredns.github.io/helm
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install CoreDNS using Helm
steps.description:
Install CoreDNS in a new namespace called 'coredns' with the following command:
```bash
helm install coredns coredns/coredns --namespace coredns --create-namespace --version v1.14.1
```

steps.title: Verify the CoreDNS installation
steps.description:
Check if the CoreDNS pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace coredns
```

steps.title: Expose CoreDNS service for external access
steps.description:
To access CoreDNS externally, expose it using a LoadBalancer service:
```bash
kubectl expose deployment coredns --type=LoadBalancer --name=coredns-external --namespace coredns --port=53 --target-port=53
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of CoreDNS will show the CoreDNS pods in a running state when you execute `kubectl get pods --namespace coredns`. You will also have resource limits configured and an external service exposed for DNS queries.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add coredns https://coredns.github.io/helm
helm repo update
helm install coredns coredns/coredns --namespace coredns --create-namespace --version v1.14.1

resources:
  limits:
    memory: "512Mi"
    cpu: "500m"
  requests:
    memory: "256Mi"
    cpu: "250m"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the CoreDNS Helm release from the 'coredns' namespace with the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall coredns --namespace coredns
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) associated with CoreDNS. Use the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace coredns
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep coredns | awk '{print $1}')
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'coredns' namespace and verify its removal with the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace coredns
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current CoreDNS configuration and any relevant data. Use the following command to export the current deployment:
```bash
kubectl get deployment coredns --namespace coredns -o yaml > coredns-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade CoreDNS to the latest version with the following commands:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade coredns coredns/coredns --namespace coredns --version v1.14.2
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check if the CoreDNS pods are running the new version successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace coredns
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If CoreDNS pods are in a 'CrashLoopBackOff' state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace coredns
```
Look for configuration issues or resource constraints.

troubleshooting.title: CoreDNS service not responding
troubleshooting.description:
If the CoreDNS service is not responding, verify the service configuration:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace coredns
```
Ensure that the service type is correct and that the pods are running.

troubleshooting.title: DNS queries failing
troubleshooting.description:
If DNS queries are failing, check the CoreDNS logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs -l k8s-app=kube-dns --namespace coredns
```
Investigate any errors related to DNS resolution.

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing OOMKilled
troubleshooting.description:
If pods are being killed due to out-of-memory (OOM) issues, check the resource usage:
```bash
kubectl top pods --namespace coredns
```
Consider increasing the memory limits in the deployment configuration.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
